clean_story: Invisible to radio waves.  Shapes that scatter signals. "Radar evasion" means designing objects—like planes or ships—to slip past radar like a shadow slips past light. Radar works by bouncing radio waves off targets, like shouting in a cave and listening for echoes. But some shapes don’t echo back. A flat metal sheet reflects waves like a mirror. A stealth aircraft’s angled panels act like funhouse mirrors—scattering waves in random directions, leaving nothing clear for the radar to "see. "The F-117 Nighthawk, first flown in 1981, used flat, jagged surfaces to break up waves like a disco ball scatters light. Its radar signature was smaller than a bird’s. Materials matter too. Radar-absorbing paint, mixed with tiny iron particles, soaks up waves like a black shirt absorbs sunlight. The B-2 Spirit bomber’s curved body and special coating make it nearly invisible—like a whisper in a storm. Three related subjects are electromagnetic silence, sonar deception, and thermal camouflage.
